title: Quick Reference Cheat Sheet
text:
This page summarizes the most important commands and tips from the guide.





Action
Windows
Mac




Run current line
Ctrl + Enter
Cmd + Enter


Save file
Ctrl + S
Cmd + S


Find text
Ctrl + F
Cmd + F


Comment/uncomment code
Ctrl + Shift + C
Cmd + Shift + C









Pane
Purpose




Source
Write and save code


Console
Run commands and see output


Environment
See objects you created


Files
Find files and folders


Plots
View graphs


Packages
Manage add-on tools


Help
Search for help









Goal
Command




Add numbers
2 + 3


Store a value
x <- 10


Print a value
print(x)


Check working directory
getwd()


Check if a file exists
file.exists("mydata.csv")


Import CSV
data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")


View first rows
head(data)


View structure
str(data)


Summarize data
summary(data)


View column names
names(data)


Calculate a mean
mean(data$quiz_score)


Make a frequency table
table(data$major)


Make a plot
plot(data$study_hours, data$quiz_score)







Keep your script and data file in the same project folder.
Use clear file names.
Avoid spaces in file names.
Use underscores _ instead of spaces.
Avoid saving important files only in Downloads.
Check your working directory with getwd().
Check whether R can find your file with file.exists().




Good:
first_script.R
mydata.csv
lab_report.qmd
Avoid:
Untitled1.R
my data final FINAL.csv
lab report new version 2.qmd



Use:
data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")
Then inspect it:
head(data)
str(data)
summary(data)
names(data)



Mean:
mean(data$quiz_score)
Frequency table:
table(data$major)
Scatterplot:
plot(
  data$study_hours,
  data$quiz_score,
  main = "Study Hours and Quiz Score",
  xlab = "Study Hours",
  ylab = "Quiz Score"
)
Histogram:
hist(
  data$quiz_score,
  main = "Distribution of Quiz Scores",
  xlab = "Quiz Score"
)










Problem
Possible Fix




File not found
Check getwd() and file.exists("mydata.csv")


Object not found
Run the code that creates the object


Typing mistake
Check spelling and capitalization


Missing quote
Put file names in quotation marks


Missing parenthesis
Make sure every ( has a matching )


Rendering error
Run the document from top to bottom and check earlier code


Package problem
Check spelling, install the package, then load it with library()






If R cannot find your CSV file, try this:
getwd()
file.exists("mydata.csv")
If file.exists("mydata.csv") gives:
[1] FALSE
then R cannot find the file in the current folder.
Check that:

The file is saved in your project folder.
The file name is exactly mydata.csv.
The file is not accidentally named mydata.csv.txt.
You are working in the correct folder.




Good object names:
quiz_score <- 90
study_hours <- 6
my_data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")
Avoid object names with spaces:
quiz score <- 90
study hours <- 6
my data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")




Save your script often.
Run code one small piece at a time.
Read error messages slowly.
Check spelling carefully.
Keep your data and script in the same folder.
Write short comments to explain your code.
Explain your results in sentences.




Here is a simple workflow from start to finish:
# Check where R is looking for files
getwd()

# Check whether the data file exists
file.exists("mydata.csv")

# Import the data
data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")

# Look at the data
head(data)
str(data)
summary(data)

# Calculate results
mean(data$quiz_score)
table(data$major)

# Make a plot
plot(
  data$study_hours,
  data$quiz_score,
  main = "Study Hours and Quiz Score",
  xlab = "Study Hours",
  ylab = "Quiz Score",
  pch = 19,
  col = "steelblue"
)



You now know how to complete a first basic R workflow:

Open RStudio.
Create a script.
Write code.
Save your work.
Organize your files.
Import a CSV dataset.
Inspect the data.
Calculate simple results.
Explain what the results mean.

That is a successful first R workflow!

title: 8. Getting Results
text:
Now that your data is in R, you can start getting results.
In this section, we will calculate:

A mean
A frequency table
A simple plot

First, import the data again:

data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")



The mean is the average.
Let’s calculate the average quiz score:

mean(data$quiz_score)

[1] 81.4


The $ symbol means:

Use a column from this dataset.

So:
data$quiz_score
means:

The quiz_score column inside the dataset called data.




If R gives you a number such as:
[1] 82.4
you can write:

The average quiz score is 82.4.







TipTip



Always explain results in words. Do not only show the code output.





You can also calculate the average number of study hours:

mean(data$study_hours)

[1] 6


A sentence for this result might be:

Students studied an average of about 6 hours.




A frequency table counts how many times each category appears.
Let’s count the number of students in each major:

table(data$major)


   Biology   Business Psychology 
         3          4          3 


This tells us how many students are listed for each major.



If R shows something like:
Biology     Business     Psychology
3           4            3
you can write:

There are 3 Biology students, 4 Business students, and 3 Psychology students in the dataset.




We can count attendance:

table(data$attendance)


 Absent Present 
      2       8 


This shows how many students were marked as Present or Absent.



A plot helps us see patterns.
Let’s make a scatterplot of study hours and quiz scores:

plot(
  data$study_hours,
  data$quiz_score,
  main = "Study Hours and Quiz Score",
  xlab = "Study Hours",
  ylab = "Quiz Score",
  pch = 19,
  col = "steelblue"
)








This graph shows whether students who studied more hours tended to have higher quiz scores.



We can also make a histogram of quiz scores:

hist(
  data$quiz_score,
  main = "Distribution of Quiz Scores",
  xlab = "Quiz Score",
  col = "lightblue",
  border = "white"
)








A histogram shows how values are spread out.









NotePractice



Use the dataset to answer these questions:

What is the average number of study hours?
How many students are in each attendance group?
Make a plot of sleep hours and quiz score.

Use these commands as a starting point:
mean(data$study_hours)
table(data$attendance)

plot(
  data$sleep_hours,
  data$quiz_score,
  main = "Sleep Hours and Quiz Score",
  xlab = "Sleep Hours",
  ylab = "Quiz Score"
)











NotePractice



After running your code, write one sentence for each result.
Example:

The average quiz score was 81.4.


Most students in this small dataset were present.


The scatterplot suggests that higher study hours may be related to higher quiz scores.






Good results include both the number and an explanation.
Instead of only writing:
[1] 6.2
write:

The average number of study hours was 6.2 hours.

Instead of only showing a plot, write:

The plot suggests that students who studied more hours tended to have higher quiz scores.







NoteReminder



Your goal is not only to run code. Your goal is to understand and explain what the results mean.





Answer these questions:

What does mean() calculate?
What does table() create?
What does the $ symbol do?
Why should you explain results in words?




In this section, you learned how to:

Calculate a mean.
Create a frequency table.
Make a simple scatterplot.
Make a histogram.
Explain results in plain language.

title: 6. Downloading a CSV Dataset
text:
In this section, you will download a CSV dataset.
A CSV file is a common type of data file. CSV stands for comma-separated values.
A CSV file looks like a spreadsheet, but it is saved as plain text.


For this guide, we will use a small dataset called:
mydata.csv
It contains information about imaginary students, including:

Study hours
Sleep hours
Attendance
Quiz score
Major

If you are viewing this guide as a website, use this link:
Download the sample CSV dataset






TipTip



If your browser opens the CSV instead of downloading it, right-click the link and choose Save Link As…





Save the file in your project folder.
Your folder should look like this:
r-onboarding-guide/
├── first_script.R
├── first_report.qmd
└── mydata.csv




In R, you can check whether the file exists:

file.exists("mydata.csv")

[1] TRUE


If R prints:
[1] TRUE
that means R found the file.
If R prints:
[1] FALSE
that means R did not find the file in the current working directory.






WarningCommon Mistake



The file name must match exactly.
These are different to R:
mydata.csv
MyData.csv
mydata (1).csv





If your instructor has not provided mydata.csv, you can create it yourself.

Open a plain text editor.
Copy the text below.
Save the file as mydata.csv.
Put it in your project folder.

student,study_hours,sleep_hours,attendance,quiz_score,major
Student 1,5,7,Present,78,Biology
Student 2,8,6,Present,91,Business
Student 3,3,8,Absent,65,Psychology
Student 4,6,7,Present,84,Biology
Student 5,7,6,Present,88,Business
Student 6,2,5,Absent,60,Psychology
Student 7,9,8,Present,95,Biology
Student 8,4,6,Present,72,Business
Student 9,6,7,Present,83,Psychology
Student 10,10,7,Present,98,Business






TipTip



Make sure the file name is exactly:
mydata.csv
not:
mydata.csv.txt











NotePractice




Download or create mydata.csv.
Save it in your project folder.
Run:

file.exists("mydata.csv")

Make sure the result is TRUE.






Answer these questions:

What does CSV stand for?
Where should you save mydata.csv?
What does file.exists("mydata.csv") check?
What does it mean if the result is FALSE?




In this section, you learned that:

A CSV file is a common data file.
CSV files can be opened in spreadsheet programs and R.
Your CSV file should be saved in your project folder.
file.exists() checks whether R can find the file.

title: 4. Running Code in RStudio
text:
Writing code is only the first step. You also need to run it.
In RStudio, there are several ways to run R code.


If your cursor is on a line of code, you can click the Run button.

Example:

3 + 4

[1] 7





You can also use a keyboard shortcut.



Computer
Shortcut




Windows
Ctrl + Enter


Mac
Cmd + Enter



This runs the current line of code.






TipTip



Keyboard shortcuts make your work faster once you get used to them.





You can highlight several lines of code and run only those lines.
Try highlighting this code:

x <- 10
y <- 5
x + y

[1] 15


Then press:

Ctrl + Enter on Windows
Cmd + Enter on Mac




To run the highlighted script, you can:

Open your .R file.
Highlight the code you want to run.
Click Run.
RStudio will run the highlighted code from top to bottom.








WarningImportant



R runs code from top to bottom.
If a later line depends on an earlier line, make sure the earlier line has already been run.





This works because height is created before it is used:

height <- 65
height + 2

[1] 67


This would cause an error if height had not already been created:
height + 2



In a Rmd or Quarto document, code usually appears inside a code chunk.
A code chunk looks like this:

2 + 2

[1] 4


You can run code chunks using the small green play button near the chunk.







TipTip



When you render a Rmd / Quarto document, R runs the code chunks from top to bottom.





Running selected code is useful when you are practicing or testing something.
Rendering is useful when you want to create the final document.



Action
What It Does




Run one line
Runs only the current line


Run selected lines
Runs only the code you highlight


Source script
Runs the whole .R script


Render document
Runs the whole .qmd document and creates output









NoteBeginner Note



If something works when you run one line but fails when you render, it may mean your document is missing an earlier line of code.
Remember: Quarto starts fresh and runs the document from top to bottom.







You may see an error like:
Error: object 'quiz_score' not found
This means R does not know what quiz_score is.
Usually this happens because:

You did not create the object yet.
You misspelled the object name.
You restarted R and need to run earlier code again.

Example mistake:
quiz_score + 5
Fix:
quiz_score <- 90
quiz_score + 5



You may see:
Error: unexpected symbol
This often means there is a typing mistake.
For example:
my score <- 90
R does not like spaces in object names.
Use:
my_score <- 90



You may see:
Error: could not find function "some_function"
This may mean:

The function name is misspelled.
You forgot to load a package.
The package is not installed.

For now, check your spelling first.










NotePractice



Create a new script and type:
a <- 12
b <- 3
a / b
Try running the code using:

The Run button.
The keyboard shortcut.
Highlighting all three lines.
The Source button.






Look at this code:
exam score <- 85
exam_score + 5
This code has two problems.
Try to fix it before looking below.
Corrected version:
exam_score <- 85
exam_score + 5
The first problem was the space in exam score.
The second problem was that the object name was not written the same way both times.






WarningCommon Mistake



R is very exact.
These names are different to R:
score
Score
quiz_score
quizscore





Answer these questions:

What button can you use to run one line of code?
What keyboard shortcut runs the current line?
Why does the order of code matter?
What does “object not found” usually mean?




In this section, you learned how to:

Run one line of code.
Run selected lines of code.
Run an entire script.
Run code chunks in a Quarto document.
Understand a few common error messages.

You are now ready to work with files and folders.

Runs the whole .qmd document and creates output









NoteBeginner Note



If something works when you run one line but fails when you render, it may mean your document is missing an earlier line of code.
Remember: Quarto starts fresh and runs the document from top to bottom.

title: 4. Running Code in RStudio
text:
You may see an error like:
Error: object 'quiz_score' not found
This means R does not know what quiz_score is.
Usually this happens because:

You did not create the object yet.
You misspelled the object name.
You restarted R and need to run earlier code again.

Example mistake:
quiz_score + 5
Fix:
quiz_score <- 90
quiz_score + 5



You may see:
Error: unexpected symbol
This often means there is a typing mistake.
For example:
my score <- 90
R does not like spaces in object names.
Use:
my_score <- 90



You may see:
Error: could not find function "some_function"
This may mean:

The function name is misspelled.
You forgot to load a package.
The package is not installed.

For now, check your spelling first.

The first problem was the space in exam score.

title: 2. Creating Your First R Script
text:
In this section, you will create your first R script.


An R script is a file where you write R code.
R script files end with:
.R
For example:
first_script.R
An R script is useful because it saves your code. You can run the same code again later without retyping everything.






TipTip



Use an R script when you want to save your work.
Use the Console when you want to quickly test one command.





In RStudio:

Click File.
Click New File.
Click R Script.


A blank script should open in the Source Pane.



Before we work with data, let’s try R as a calculator.
Type the following into your script:

2 + 3

[1] 5

10 - 4

[1] 6

5 * 6

[1] 30

20 / 4

[1] 5


These symbols mean:



Symbol
Meaning




+
Add


-
Subtract


*
Multiply


/
Divide









NoteTry It Yourself



Change the numbers and run the code again.
For example, try:
100 / 5
12 * 3





A variable is a name that stores a value.
For example:

age <- 18
age

[1] 18


The symbol <- means “store this value.”
So this line:
age <- 18
means:

Store the number 18 in a variable called age.




Variables help us reuse information.
For example, instead of typing the same number many times, we can give it a name.

quiz_score <- 92
homework_score <- 88

quiz_score

[1] 92

homework_score

[1] 88


Now we can use those variables in a calculation:

final_score <- (quiz_score + homework_score) / 2
final_score

[1] 90


This code calculates the average of the quiz score and homework score.



Sometimes you want to clearly print something.
You can use print():

print(final_score)

[1] 90


For simple work, typing the object name also shows the value:

final_score

[1] 90


Both are okay.






TipTip



In R, object names should not contain spaces.
Use this:
quiz_score <- 92
Not this:
quiz score <- 92





A comment is a note to yourself or your reader.
In R, comments start with #.
R ignores comments when running code.

# This stores my quiz score
quiz_score <- 92

# This prints the quiz score
quiz_score

[1] 92


Comments are useful because they help explain what your code is doing.



To save your script:

Click File.
Click Save.
Name your file:

first_script.R

Save it in your project folder.


Show the Save button and file name first_script.R.






WarningCommon Mistake



Do not save your script with a name like:
Untitled1.R
Use a clear name, such as:
first_script.R

Create a new R script and write code that:

Stores your favorite number in a variable.
Stores your age in another variable.
Adds the two numbers together.
Prints the result.

Example:
favorite_number <- 7
age <- 18
result <- favorite_number + age
print(result)





Answer these questions in your own words:

What is an R script?
Why is it useful to save code in a script?
What does <- do?
What is the difference between these two lines?

score <- 90
print(score)

Use R for basic arithmetic.
Store values in variables.
Print results.
Add comments.
Save your script.

You have now written your first R code!

title: Welcome
text:
Welcome to the BU R Onboarding Guide!
This guide is for students who are using R and RStudio for the first time. You do not need any previous programming experience. We will go step by step.
By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

Open RStudio.
Understand the main parts of the RStudio screen.
Write and run simple R code.
Save your work in an R script.
Understand the difference between R scripts, R Markdown, and Quarto documents.
Organize your project files.
Import a CSV dataset into R.
View and summarize your data.
Create simple results such as means, tables, and plots.



R is a programming language used for working with data.
You can use R to:

Store data.
Clean data.
Summarize data.
Make graphs.
Run statistical analyses.
Create reports.

In this course, we will use R because it helps us work carefully and clearly with data.



RStudio is a program that makes R easier to use.
Think of R as the engine, and RStudio as the dashboard. R does the calculations, while RStudio gives you buttons, panels, files, and tools that make your work easier to manage.






TipTip



You need both R and RStudio installed.

R does the actual computing.
RStudio gives you a friendly place to write and run R code.




R download - available for Windows/Mac/Linux
RStudio Desktop download - available for Windows/Mac/Linux

Make sure you install R first, then RStudio. RStudio will ask you to locate your R installation when you open it for the first time.






We are using R because it helps you learn how to work with real data.
Instead of only doing calculations by hand, you will learn how to:

Ask questions about data.
Use code to answer those questions.
Check your work.
Share your results.

You do not need to become a professional programmer. The goal is to become comfortable using R as a tool for learning and Statistical data analysis.



After completing this onboarding guide, you should be able to:

Identify the main parts of RStudio.
Create and save an R script.
Run basic R commands.
Explain what a Quarto document is.
Set up a simple project folder.
Download and import a CSV file.
Use basic commands to inspect a dataset.
Calculate a mean, make a frequency table, and create a simple plot.







NoteBefore You Start



It is okay if you make mistakes. Everyone does when learning R.
A big part of learning R is reading error messages, fixing small problems, and trying again.





Go to 1. Getting Familiar with RStudio.

title: 1. Getting Familiar with RStudio
text:
When you open RStudio, you will see several panels. Each panel has a different job.
At first, the screen may look busy. That is normal. You do not need to understand everything right away.


RStudio usually has four main areas:

Source Pane
Console
Environment Pane
Output Pane - Files / Plots / Packages / Help Pane




Source: Posit PBC documentation (https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/ide/get-started/)





The Source Pane is where you write and save code.
You will usually write your code in a file called an R script. R scripts end with .R.
Example file name:
first_script.R
The Source Pane is useful because it lets you save your work and come back to it later.






TipTip



The Source Pane is like a notebook for your code.





The Console is where R runs commands.
You can type code directly into the Console and press Enter.
For example:
2 + 2
R will show the answer:
[1] 4
The Console is useful for quick tests, but you usually should save important code in an R script.






WarningCommon Mistake



Do not only type important work into the Console. If you close RStudio, that code may be hard to find later.
Use an R script to save your work.





The Environment Pane shows objects you have created.
An object is something stored in R, such as a number, a word, or a dataset.
For example, if you run:
score <- 95
You should see score appear in the Environment Pane.



This pane has several useful tabs.


The Files tab shows folders and files on your computer.
You can use it to find your project folder and open files.



The Plots tab shows graphs that you create in R.



The Packages tab shows extra R tools that are installed.
A package is like an add-on for R.



The Help tab lets you search for information about R commands.
For example, you can search for help about the mean() function.










NotePractice



Open RStudio and try the following:

Find the Console.
Type this command:

10 + 5

Press Enter.
Look for the answer.
Find the Environment Pane.
Find the Files tab.






Answer these questions in your own words:

Where do you write and save code?
Where does R show the results of commands?
Where can you see objects you created?
Where do plots appear?




In this section, you learned that RStudio has several main panes:

The Source Pane is where you write and save code.
The Console is where code runs and results appear.
The Environment Pane shows objects you created.
The Files / Plots / Packages / Help Pane helps you manage files, view graphs, use packages, and get help.

title: 3. What is an R Markdown / Quarto Document?
text:
So far, we have used an R script to write code.
Now we will learn about documents that combine:

Regular writing
R code
Results
Graphs

These documents are useful for creating reports.


A reproducible report combines your explanation and your code in one place.
This is helpful because someone else can see:

What question you asked.
What code you used.
What results you got.
How you explained those results.







TipTip



A reproducible report helps you avoid copying and pasting results by hand.













File Type
Name
What It Is Used For




.R
R script
Writing and saving R code


.Rmd
R Markdown document
Combining text, R code, and output


.qmd
Quarto document
A newer format for reports, websites, slides, and more






R Markdown is a document format that lets you combine writing, code, and results.
R Markdown files usually end with:
.Rmd
R Markdown has been widely used in many statistics and data science courses.



Quarto is a newer tool for creating documents that include code and results.
Quarto files usually end with:
.qmd
With Quarto, you can create:

HTML webpages
PDF documents
Word documents
Slides
Websites

This guide is written as a Quarto website.






NoteBeginner Note



You do not need to memorize all the differences between R Markdown and Quarto right now.
For this course, focus on this idea:

A Quarto document lets you write explanations, run code, and show results in one file.






A simple Quarto document usually has:

A title section at the top.
Regular text.
Code chunks.




At the top of a Rmd/Quarto file, you may see something like this:
---
title: "My First Rmd Document"
author: "Your Name"
format: html
---
This part is called the YAML header.
You do not need to write complicated YAML right now. It simply tells Rmd basic information about the document.



You can type normal sentences in a Rmd document.
For example:
This report shows my first analysis in R.



A code chunk is a special section where R code can be run.
In a real Quarto document, an R code chunk has three parts:

A starting line with three backticks followed by {r}.
One or more lines of R code.
An ending line with three backticks.

For example, a code chunk that calculates 2 + 2 would look like this in your .Rmd file:
Line 1: three backticks followed by {r}
Line 2: 2 + 2
Line 3: three backticks
When you render the document, Quarto runs the code and shows the result.






TipTip



Code chunks are different from regular text.
Regular text explains your work.
Code chunks run R code.





Here is the structure of a small Quarto document:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
author: "Your Name"
format: html
---

# My First Quarto Document

This is my first Quarto document.

Here is a simple calculation.

Then add an R code chunk that contains:

2 + 2

After rendering, the answer appears below the code.
In your actual .qmd file, the R code chunk would start with three backticks followed by {r}, and end with three backticks.



To render a document means to turn it into a finished report.
For example, In Quarto and R Markdown, .qmd or .Rmd file can turn into:

an HTML webpage,
a PDF document,
or a Word document.

In RStudio, you can usually click the Render button.



When you open a .Rmd or .qmd file in RStudio, you will see:


Click Source to see the raw text and code in your document. 



Click Visual to see a more polished version of your document, with formatted text and rendered code chunks. 



 Click Show in new window to see the rendered document in a separate window.

Code chunks are different from regular text.
Regular text explains your work.
Code chunks run R code.

title: 3. What is an R Markdown / Quarto Document?
text:
Here is the structure of a small Quarto document:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
author: "Your Name"
format: html
---

# My First Quarto Document

This is my first Quarto document.

Here is a simple calculation.

Then add an R code chunk that contains:

2 + 2

After rendering, the answer appears below the code.
In your actual .qmd file, the R code chunk would start with three backticks followed by {r}, and end with three backticks.

title: 3. What is an R Markdown / Quarto Document?
text:
To render a document means to turn it into a finished report.
For example, In Quarto and R Markdown, .qmd or .Rmd file can turn into:

an HTML webpage,
a PDF document,
or a Word document.

In RStudio, you can usually click the Render button.

title: 3. What is an R Markdown / Quarto Document?
text:
When you open a .Rmd or .qmd file in RStudio, you will see:


Click Source to see the raw text and code in your document. 



Click Visual to see a more polished version of your document, with formatted text and rendered code chunks. 



 Click Show in new window to see the rendered document in a separate window.

title: 5. Working with Files and Folders
text:
When working with data, file organization matters.
If your files are organized, R can find them more easily.


The working directory is the folder where R is currently looking for files.
If you ask R to open a file, R first looks in the working directory.
For example:
read.csv("mydata.csv")
This tells R:

Look in the current working directory for a file called mydata.csv.




You can check your working directory with:

getwd()

[1] "/Users/ylim2/Documents/01_Work/01_MA213/R_onboarding/R_onboarding_repo/R_Onboarding"


This prints the folder where R is currently working.






TipTip



If R cannot find your file, checking getwd() is often the first thing to do.






Good file names are short and clear.
Good examples:
mydata.csv
first_script.R
lab_report.qmd
Less helpful examples:
Untitled1.R
final final data copy.csv
my data file.csv






TipTip



Avoid spaces in file names.
Use underscores _ instead:
my_data.csv
lab_report.qmd











NotePractice




Create a folder for this course.
Inside it, create a folder for this assignment or lab.
Save your R script in that folder.
In RStudio, run:

getwd()

Check whether R is working in the folder you expected.












WarningWarning



Avoid saving course files only in your Downloads folder.
The Downloads folder can become messy quickly, and files may be hard to find later.





Answer these questions:

What is a working directory?
Why is it helpful to keep your script and data file in the same folder?
What command shows the current working directory?
Why should you avoid unclear file names?




In this section, you learned that:

The working directory is where R looks for files.
getwd() shows your current working directory. 
Clear file names help prevent mistakes. `````

title: 7. Importing Data into R
text:
Now that the CSV file is saved in your project folder, we can import it into R.
To import data means to bring data from a file into R so we can work with it.


Use this command:

data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")

Let’s break it down.



Part
Meaning




data
The name we are giving the dataset in R


<-
Stores something in an object


read.csv()
A function that reads CSV files


"mydata.csv"
The file name



So this line means:

Read the file called mydata.csv and store it in an object called data.







TipTip



The file name must be inside quotation marks.





Use head() to see the first few rows:

head(data)

    student study_hours sleep_hours attendance quiz_score      major
1 Student 1           5           7    Present         78    Biology
2 Student 2           8           6    Present         91   Business
3 Student 3           3           8     Absent         65 Psychology
4 Student 4           6           7    Present         84    Biology
5 Student 5           7           6    Present         88   Business
6 Student 6           2           5     Absent         60 Psychology


This helps you quickly check that the data imported correctly.



Use str() to see the structure of the dataset:

str(data)

'data.frame':   10 obs. of  6 variables:
 $ student    : chr  "Student 1" "Student 2" "Student 3" "Student 4" ...
 $ study_hours: int  5 8 3 6 7 2 9 4 6 10
 $ sleep_hours: int  7 6 8 7 6 5 8 6 7 7
 $ attendance : chr  "Present" "Present" "Absent" "Present" ...
 $ quiz_score : int  78 91 65 84 88 60 95 72 83 98
 $ major      : chr  "Biology" "Business" "Psychology" "Biology" ...


This tells you:

How many rows there are.
How many columns there are.
The names of the columns.
The type of information in each column.

For example:

Numbers may be shown as int or num.
Words or categories may be shown as chr.




Use summary() to get a quick summary:

summary(data)

   student           study_hours     sleep_hours   attendance       
 Length:10          Min.   : 2.00   Min.   :5.0   Length:10         
 Class :character   1st Qu.: 4.25   1st Qu.:6.0   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Median : 6.00   Median :7.0   Mode  :character  
                    Mean   : 6.00   Mean   :6.7                     
                    3rd Qu.: 7.75   3rd Qu.:7.0                     
                    Max.   :10.00   Max.   :8.0                     
   quiz_score       major          
 Min.   :60.00   Length:10         
 1st Qu.:73.50   Class :character  
 Median :83.50   Mode  :character  
 Mean   :81.40                     
 3rd Qu.:90.25                     
 Max.   :98.00                     


For number columns, R may show:

Minimum
Median
Mean
Maximum

For text columns, R may show basic information about the values.



You can see the column names with:

names(data)

[1] "student"     "study_hours" "sleep_hours" "attendance"  "quiz_score" 
[6] "major"      





If you run:
head(data)
you are asking:

Show me the first few students in the dataset.

If you run:
str(data)
you are asking:

What kind of information is stored in each column?

If you run:
summary(data)
you are asking:

Give me a quick overview of the dataset.










NotePractice



Import the dataset and answer these questions:

How many columns are in the dataset?
What are the column names?
What is one number column?
What is one category or text column?

Use these commands:
data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")
head(data)
str(data)
summary(data)
names(data)











WarningWarning



This will not work if the file is not in your working directory:
data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")
If you get a file not found error, go back to Section 5 and check your working directory.





Answer these questions:

What does it mean to import data?
What does read.csv() do?
Why does "mydata.csv" need quotation marks?
What does head(data) show?
What does summary(data) show?




In this section, you learned how to:

Import a CSV file into R.
Store the dataset in an object called data.
View the first few rows.
Check the structure of the dataset.
Summarize the dataset.
View column names.

title: Common Problems and Solutions
text:
Everyone gets errors in R. Errors are normal.
The important skill is learning how to read the message and try a fix.




Error in file(file, "rt") : cannot open the connection
or:
No such file or directory



R cannot find your file.



Run:

getwd()

[1] "/Users/ylim2/Documents/01_Work/01_MA213/R_onboarding/R_onboarding_repo/R_Onboarding"


Then check whether mydata.csv is inside that folder.
Also run:

file.exists("mydata.csv")

[1] TRUE


If the result is FALSE, R cannot find the file.




Move mydata.csv into your project folder.
Check the spelling of the file name.
Make sure the file ends in .csv.
Make sure you are working in the correct folder.







WarningCommon Mistake



mydata.csv and mydata (1).csv are different file names.






Sometimes your instructor may ask you to install a package.
Example:
install.packages("ggplot2")



Internet connection problems.
Typing the package name incorrectly.
R asks you to restart.
You do not have permission to install software on the computer.





Check your internet connection.
Make sure the package name is inside quotation marks.
Restart RStudio and try again.
Ask your instructor or campus IT for help if you are on a lab computer.







TipTip



You only need to install a package once on your computer.
But you need to load it with library() each time you start a new R session.






R is very picky about spelling.
These are all different to R:
quiz_score
Quiz_score
quizscore
quiz.score










Mistake
Example
Fix




Missing quote
read.csv(mydata.csv)
read.csv("mydata.csv")


Misspelled object
quz_score
quiz_score


Missing parenthesis
mean(data$quiz_score
mean(data$quiz_score)


Wrong capitalization
Read.csv()
read.csv()







If R is looking in the wrong folder, it may not find your files.
Check the working directory with:

getwd()

[1] "/Users/ylim2/Documents/01_Work/01_MA213/R_onboarding/R_onboarding_repo/R_Onboarding"


In RStudio, using an RStudio Project can help keep your working directory organized.






TipTip



For each assignment or project, keep your script and data file in the same folder.





When rendering a Quarto document, R runs the code from top to bottom.
A document may fail to render if:

A file is missing.
A package is not loaded.
An object is used before it is created.
There is a typing mistake in a code chunk.



This can fail:
mean(data$quiz_score)
if data was never created earlier in the document.



Make sure this line appears first:
data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")






Error: object 'data' not found



R does not know what data is.



Create the object first:
data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")
Then run your analysis.




This is incorrect:
read.csv(mydata.csv)
This is correct:
read.csv("mydata.csv")
File names must be in quotation marks.



When something goes wrong, ask:

Did I spell everything correctly?
Did I use quotation marks around file names?
Did I run the earlier lines of code?
Is my file in the right folder?
Does file.exists("mydata.csv") return TRUE?
Did I close all parentheses?
Did I restart RStudio and try again?







NoteEncouragement



Errors do not mean you are bad at R.
Errors are part of learning. Read the message, try one fix, and run the code again.











NotePractice



For each example, decide what the problem is.


read.csv(mydata.csv)
Question: What is missing?



mean(data$quiz_score
Question: What is missing?



Data <- read.csv("mydata.csv")
head(data)
Question: Why might this fail?







Example 1 is missing quotation marks around the file name.
Example 2 is missing a closing parenthesis.
Example 3 may fail because Data and data are different names in R.




In this section, you learned how to troubleshoot common problems, including:

File not found errors.
Package installation issues.
Typing mistakes.
Working directory problems.
Rendering errors.
Object not found errors.
